content.text: Computing infrastructure has shifted from physical data centers owned by individual organizations to shared, on-demand pools of resources delivered over the internet. This shift — called cloud computing — changes not just where workloads run, but how teams build, scale, and pay for software.
What It Is

Cloud computing is the on-demand delivery of IT resources — compute, storage, databases, networking, analytics, machine learning, and more — over the internet with pay-as-you-go pricing. Instead of buying, owning, and maintaining physical servers and data centers, organizations rent access to resources from a cloud provider and pay only for what they consume.

The National Institute of Standards and Technology (NIST) defines cloud computing through five essential characteristics: on-demand self-service, broad network access, resource pooling, rapid elasticity, and measured service. These characteristics distinguish cloud computing from traditional managed hosting or outsourced IT — the user provisions and releases resources unilaterally, without human interaction with the provider, and the system automatically measures and controls resource usage.

Cloud providers — AWS, Microsoft Azure, Google Cloud Platform, and others — operate massive global networks of data centers. They achieve economies of scale unavailable to individual organizations, passing those cost and reliability benefits on through competitive pricing and service-level agreements. For AWS specifically, this infrastructure spans dozens of geographic regions, each containing multiple isolated Availability Zones connected by a private, high-bandwidth global network.
Why It Matters

Before cloud computing, deploying new infrastructure meant weeks or months of lead time: researching hardware, issuing purchase orders, receiving equipment, racking and cabling servers, installing operating systems, and configuring networking. A startup needing to handle a sudden traffic surge had no good options — the hardware was either not there, or it had been provisioned in advance at great expense and mostly sat idle.

Cloud computing solves three fundamental problems in infrastructure management. First, it eliminates the capacity planning guessing game: organizations no longer have to decide years in advance how much hardware to buy, and then live with either waste (over-provisioned) or bottlenecks (under-provisioned). Second, it dramatically reduces time-to-value — a developer can spin up a database, object store, or a fleet of virtual machines in minutes using a web console or CLI. Third, it transfers operational burden: instead of employing teams to manage physical hardware, power, cooling, and low-level networking, teams focus on the applications and services that differentiate their business.

These changes matter particularly for AWS learners because AWS pioneered many of the service categories now standard in cloud computing. Understanding the foundational model is essential for making good architectural decisions: knowing why cloud pricing works the way it does, why services are spread across regions and Availability Zones, and how the shared responsibility model allocates security tasks between AWS and the customer.
Benefits
Elasticity and agility.

Resources scale up or down in response to demand. A retail site can handle Black Friday traffic without permanently provisioning for that peak. Cloud elasticity is measured in minutes, not procurement cycles.
Variable cost model.
Capital expenditure (CapEx) for hardware becomes operational expenditure (OpEx) billed by consumption. Cash is not tied up in depreciating assets, and costs track actual usage.
Global reach.

AWS operates regions across North America, Europe, Asia-Pacific, South America, and the Middle East. Deploying to a region near end users reduces latency without requiring physical presence in those geographies.
Managed services.

Providers operate the underlying infrastructure, apply security patches, and handle hardware failures. This frees teams from undifferentiated heavy lifting — tasks like disk replacement or OS patching that add no value to the end product.
High availability by design.

Cloud providers build redundancy into their infrastructure. Availability Zones (AZs) are physically separate data centers within a region. Deploying across multiple AZs provides fault tolerance that would be prohibitively expensive to replicate on-premises.
Security investment at scale.
AWS employs thousands of security engineers and maintains compliance certifications (SOC 2, ISO 27001, PCI DSS, HIPAA eligibility, FedRAMP) that most organizations could not achieve independently.
Speed of experimentation.
Spinning up a new environment for testing, a proof-of-concept, or a new product line takes minutes. Failed experiments can be torn down immediately, with cost limited to hours of usage.
Trade-offs
Cloud computing introduces real trade-offs that must be understood before committing to an architecture or a provider.
Operational cost at scale.

Pay-as-you-go pricing is efficient at low to moderate utilization, but at very high, sustained utilization — especially for compute — it can exceed the amortized cost of owned hardware. Companies running tens of thousands of steady-state servers sometimes repatriate workloads.
Vendor lock-in.
Using proprietary managed services (DynamoDB, Kinesis, SageMaker) accelerates development but ties workloads to AWS. Migrating later requires re-engineering around different APIs and data models.
Shared responsibility complexity.

Security responsibilities are split between AWS (the cloud) and the customer (in the cloud). Misconfigured S3 buckets, overly permissive IAM roles, and unpatched guest OS instances are common causes of breaches — and they are always the customer's responsibility.
Internet dependency.

Cloud resources are reached over the internet or dedicated connections (AWS Direct Connect). Latency-sensitive workloads — real-time industrial control, certain financial trading systems — may not tolerate that path.
Data residency and compliance.

Some regulations (GDPR, data localization laws in certain countries) constrain where data can be stored and processed. While AWS provides region-level isolation, compliance must be validated per service and configuration.
Noisy neighbor effects.

On shared hypervisors or shared storage systems, neighboring tenants can affect performance. AWS largely mitigates this with dedicated instance types and Nitro hypervisors, but it cannot be completely eliminated on all service tiers.
How It Works

At its core, cloud computing is built on virtualization — the ability to run multiple isolated virtual machines (VMs) on a single physical host. A hypervisor partitions the host's CPU, memory, disk I/O, and network bandwidth among VMs, each of which behaves as an independent computer. The cloud provider's control plane manages thousands of hypervisors, tracking resource availability and placing new VMs on hosts with sufficient capacity.

Resource pooling — one of the five NIST characteristics — means that compute, storage, and network capacity is shared across many customers using a multi-tenant model. The cloud provider dynamically assigns resources to customers based on demand. No single customer owns a specific server; instead, they own a logical resource (an EC2 instance, an S3 bucket) that is backed by physical infrastructure managed by the provider.

AWS uses its own Nitro System — a combination of purpose-built hardware (Nitro Cards) and a lightweight hypervisor — to deliver near bare-metal performance for virtualized instances. The Nitro Cards offload I/O processing (network, storage) from the host CPU to dedicated hardware, so the full capacity of the host's CPU and memory is available to the customer's workload. This is a meaningful technical advantage over software-only hypervisors like Xen or QEMU.

Cloud storage works on a similar pooling model. Amazon S3, for example, is an object store backed by a distributed system of commodity disks with data striped, replicated, and indexed across many drives and hosts. The API presents a simple key-value interface; the underlying distribution is invisible to the customer. Durability guarantees (S3 Standard offers 99.999999999% — eleven nines — annual object durability) are achieved through redundant storage across multiple facilities.

Measured service — billing by consumption — is implemented through per-resource metering. Every API call, GB of storage, GB of data transfer, and CPU-hour is metered and recorded. AWS bills hourly or per-second for EC2 instances, per-request and per-GB for S3, and per-connection-hour for load balancers, among hundreds of other dimensions. This granularity means workloads are charged in proportion to actual use, but also means cost modeling must account for all dimensions, not just the most visible ones.
Architecture: Service Models and Shared Responsibility

Cloud computing services are grouped into three delivery models — IaaS, PaaS, and SaaS — that differ in how much of the infrastructure stack the provider manages. The diagram below compares these against an on-premises deployment, highlighting which layers you manage and which the cloud provider manages:

attrs.alt: Cloud service models: IaaS, PaaS, SaaS compared to on-premises

content.text: Infrastructure as a Service (IaaS)

IaaS provides virtualized computing resources over the internet. The provider manages physical servers, storage, and networking; the customer manages the operating system, runtime, middleware, data, and applications. AWS EC2, EBS (block storage), VPC (virtual networking), and S3 are IaaS offerings. IaaS gives maximum control and flexibility — you choose the OS, install your own software stack, and tune the kernel — at the cost of bearing more operational responsibility.
Platform as a Service (PaaS)

PaaS provides a managed platform for deploying applications. The provider manages the OS, runtime, patching, and scaling infrastructure; the customer manages only the application code and its configuration. AWS Elastic Beanstalk, AWS Lambda (for functions), and AWS RDS (managed relational databases) fit the PaaS model. Teams deploying on PaaS write application code without managing servers, but have less control over the underlying environment.
Software as a Service (SaaS)

SaaS delivers fully managed software applications over the internet. The provider manages everything — hardware, OS, application, updates, and security patching. The customer configures the application through a UI or API but does not manage any infrastructure. Examples include Salesforce, Gmail, and AWS services consumed by end users such as Amazon WorkDocs or Amazon Chime.
When to Use It
Cloud computing is the right choice in most greenfield scenarios and many migration scenarios. Key indicators that cloud is a strong fit:
Workloads with variable or unpredictable traffic (e-commerce, event-driven applications, batch jobs)
New applications where time-to-market is a competitive factor
Teams that lack the budget or expertise to operate physical data centers
Applications that need global distribution without the cost of building regional data centers
Development and test environments that are only needed part of the time
Machine learning and big data workloads that require massive, temporary bursts of compute

Cloud is a weaker fit when workloads are large, steady-state, and performance-sensitive with latency requirements below what the internet can reliably deliver; when regulatory constraints prohibit public cloud (though private cloud deployments using tools like VMware Cloud or AWS GovCloud address many of these); or when the organization has already made a significant, long-lived hardware investment that is not yet depreciated.
How to Implement: Getting Started on AWS

The entry point to AWS is an AWS account. Every account has a root user (the email and password used to create the account) and starts with access to the free tier — a set of services that are free up to a monthly usage limit for the first 12 months, designed for learning and experimentation.
Step 1: Create and Secure an Account
Create an AWS account at aws.amazon.com. A credit card is required, but free-tier usage incurs no charges.
Immediately enable MFA on the root account. The root user has unrestricted access and must be protected.
Create an IAM user for day-to-day work. Do not use the root account for routine tasks.
Install the AWS CLI and configure it with the IAM user's access keys:

# Install the AWS CLI (macOS example via Homebrew)
brew install awscli

# Configure with your IAM credentials
aws configure
# AWS Access Key ID: <your-key-id>
# AWS Secret Access Key: <your-secret>
# Default region name: us-east-1
# Default output format: json

# Verify the CLI is working
aws sts get-caller-identity
Step 2: Launch a Simple Resource
The simplest cloud resource to launch is an S3 bucket — a globally unique, managed object store. This demonstrates the core cloud workflow: provision a resource via API, use it, and release it.

# Create an S3 bucket (bucket names must be globally unique)
aws s3 mb s3://my-first-cloud-bucket-$(date +%s) --region us-east-1

# Upload a file
echo 'Hello, Cloud!' > hello.txt
aws s3 cp hello.txt s3://my-first-cloud-bucket-<timestamp>/hello.txt

# List objects in the bucket
aws s3 ls s3://my-first-cloud-bucket-<timestamp>/

# Download the file
aws s3 cp s3://my-first-cloud-bucket-<timestamp>/hello.txt downloaded.txt
cat downloaded.txt

# Delete the bucket and its contents when done
aws s3 rm s3://my-first-cloud-bucket-<timestamp>/ --recursive
aws s3 rb s3://my-first-cloud-bucket-<timestamp>
Examples
Example 1: Launching a Virtual Server (EC2)
The most direct IaaS experience on AWS is launching an EC2 instance — a virtual machine. The following CLI command launches a free-tier-eligible instance running Amazon Linux 2:

# Find the latest Amazon Linux 2 AMI in your region
aws ec2 describe-images \
  --owners amazon \
  --filters 'Name=name,Values=amzn2-ami-hvm-*-x86_64-gp2' \
             'Name=state,Values=available' \
  --query 'sort_by(Images, &CreationDate)[-1].ImageId' \
  --output text

# Launch a t2.micro instance (free tier eligible)
aws ec2 run-instances \
  --image-id ami-0abcdef1234567890 \
  --instance-type t2.micro \
  --key-name my-key-pair \
  --security-group-ids sg-12345678 \
  --subnet-id subnet-12345678 \
  --count 1 \
  --tag-specifications 'ResourceType=instance,Tags=[{Key=Name,Value=my-first-ec2}]'

# Check instance status
aws ec2 describe-instances \
  --filters 'Name=tag:Name,Values=my-first-ec2' \
  --query 'Reservations[0].Instances[0].{ID:InstanceId,State:State.Name,IP:PublicIpAddress}'

# Terminate when done (stops billing)
aws ec2 terminate-instances --instance-ids i-0123456789abcdef0
Example 2: Serverless Function (PaaS / FaaS)

AWS Lambda demonstrates the PaaS model at its most abstract — write a function, deploy it, and AWS handles all infrastructure. The following is a complete Lambda function in Python that returns the current UTC time:

# lambda_function.py
import json
from datetime import datetime, timezone

def lambda_handler(event, context):
    """
    A minimal Lambda function. AWS provides the runtime, container,
    networking, and scaling — the developer only writes this handler.
    """
    now = datetime.now(timezone.utc).isoformat()
    return {
        'statusCode': 200,
        'body': json.dumps({
            'message': 'Hello from the cloud',
            'timestamp': now,
            'region': context.invoked_function_arn.split(':')[3]
        })
    }

# Package and deploy the function
zip function.zip lambda_function.py

aws lambda create-function \
  --function-name hello-cloud \
  --runtime python3.12 \
  --role arn:aws:iam::123456789012:role/lambda-basic-role \
  --handler lambda_function.lambda_handler \
  --zip-file fileb://function.zip

# Invoke it immediately
aws lambda invoke \
  --function-name hello-cloud \
  --payload '{}' \
  response.json

cat response.json
Real-World Example

A media company runs a video transcoding pipeline on AWS to process uploaded videos at variable scale. When a user uploads a video, the file lands in S3, which triggers an SQS message. An Auto Scaling Group of EC2 instances (running ffmpeg) picks up jobs from SQS, transcodes the video into multiple resolutions, and saves the outputs to S3. CloudFront (CDN) delivers the transcoded video to end users globally with low latency.

During peak hours (evenings in major markets), the Auto Scaling Group might run 200 instances. At 3 AM on a weekday, it might scale down to two instances. The cost tracks actual usage — no capacity is wasted. An equivalent on-premises deployment would require provisioning for peak capacity permanently, leaving 190 servers idle for most of the day. The cloud model reduces the compute cost for this workload by roughly 90% by eliminating idle capacity.

This architecture also benefits from resilience: S3 is 11-nines durable, SQS messages are retained for up to 14 days if consumers are unavailable, and the EC2 instances can span multiple Availability Zones. If one AZ loses power, transcoding continues in the others without manual intervention.
Common Mistakes
Treating cloud like a hosting provider

A common pattern when migrating to cloud is "lift and shift" — moving a VM running a monolith to a single EC2 instance. This removes CapEx but preserves all the operational overhead of managing a server. It also forgoes elasticity, managed services, and the resilience patterns that make cloud architecture valuable. Lift-and-shift is sometimes a valid intermediate step, but treating it as the end goal misses most of the cloud's value.
Leaving resources running

Unlike on-premises hardware, cloud resources cost money every hour they exist. EC2 instances left running over a weekend after a demo, RDS databases never deleted after a project ends, and NAT Gateways with no traffic are common sources of unexpected bills. Use AWS Cost Explorer, billing alerts, and tagging policies to track resource usage.
Using the root account for daily work

The AWS root account has unrestricted access and cannot have its permissions limited by IAM policies. Compromising it gives an attacker complete control over the account. The root account should be locked behind MFA and only used for the handful of tasks that require it (changing the account's email, enabling certain account features). All routine work should use IAM users or IAM roles with least-privilege permissions.
Assuming one region is enough

A single AWS region (e.g., us-east-1) can and does experience outages affecting multiple services simultaneously. Production systems that require high availability should span at least two Availability Zones within a region; systems with extreme availability requirements (or global user bases) may need multi-region architectures with active-active or active-passive failover.
Best Practices
Apply the AWS Well-Architected Framework.

AWS's Well-Architected Framework defines six pillars: Operational Excellence, Security, Reliability, Performance Efficiency, Cost Optimization, and Sustainability. Use the Well-Architected Tool in the console to review workloads against these pillars.
Design for failure.
Assume individual components will fail and design systems to continue operating. Use multiple AZs, health checks, auto-recovery, and circuit breakers.
Use infrastructure as code.
Define and provision resources using AWS CloudFormation, CDK, or Terraform rather than clicking through the console. IaC is reproducible, version-controlled, and reviewable.
Tag every resource.
Tags (key-value metadata attached to resources) enable cost allocation by project, environment, or team; support access control policies; and make cleanup audits practical.
Start with managed services.
Prefer RDS over self-managed MySQL on EC2, EKS over self-managed Kubernetes, ElastiCache over self-managed Redis. Managed services shift operational burden to AWS for common components.
Right-size from the start.
Choose instance types based on measured workload requirements, not maximum possible load. AWS Compute Optimizer analyzes CloudWatch metrics and recommends right-sized instances.
Performance and Scalability

Cloud infrastructure provides two fundamental scaling axes. Vertical scaling (scaling up) means upgrading to a larger instance type — more CPU, more RAM. On AWS, this requires stopping the instance, changing the instance type, and restarting. It is fast and simple but has a ceiling (the largest EC2 instance is currently the u-24tb1.metal with 448 vCPUs and 24 TB of memory).

Horizontal scaling (scaling out) means adding more instances of the same type behind a load balancer. AWS Auto Scaling Groups automate this: define minimum, desired, and maximum instance counts along with scaling policies (CPU utilization, request count per target, custom CloudWatch metrics), and the group adds or removes instances automatically. Horizontal scaling is preferred for stateless workloads — it has no ceiling and enables zero-downtime scaling events.

Network performance scales with instance type on AWS. Larger instances have higher baseline network bandwidth and can qualify for Elastic Fabric Adapter (EFA), enabling extremely low-latency, high-throughput communication suitable for tightly-coupled HPC workloads. For storage, EBS volume throughput and IOPS are provisioned independently of instance type, allowing fine-grained I/O performance tuning.
Tooling and Ecosystem
AWS provides multiple surfaces for interacting with its services, each suited to different workflows:
AWS Management Console.
A web-based GUI for exploring services, running one-off tasks, and visualizing the account. Good for learning but unsuitable for repeatable operations.
AWS CLI.
A command-line tool for all AWS APIs. Scriptable, pipeable, and the basis of many automation workflows. Installable via pip, Homebrew, or platform packages.
AWS SDKs.
Official SDKs exist for Python (boto3), JavaScript/TypeScript, Java, Go, .NET, Ruby, and others. SDKs handle authentication, retries, and pagination.
AWS CloudFormation / CDK.
Infrastructure-as-code tools. CloudFormation accepts YAML/JSON templates; CDK lets you define stacks in TypeScript, Python, Java, or Go and synthesizes CloudFormation.
Terraform.
A provider-agnostic IaC tool widely used for AWS. Maintains a state file and plans changes before applying them, enabling safe incremental modifications.
AWS CloudShell.
A browser-based shell pre-authenticated with your AWS credentials. No local CLI installation required; useful for quick queries from any machine.
Comparison with Alternatives
Approach

Best for

OpEx, pay-per-use
Elastic, near-unlimited
Low (managed services)
Variable workloads, startups, global reach

CapEx + OpEx
Fixed (procurement required)
High (all layers)
Regulatory constraints, latency-critical, steady-state high-utilization

CapEx + OpEx (higher)
Elastic within owned hardware
Medium–High
Regulated industries needing cloud patterns on owned hardware
Hybrid Cloud

Cloud bursting possible
High (two environments)
Gradual migration, data residency + cloud agility needed simultaneously
Security and Reliability
Shared Responsibility Model
AWS operates under a shared responsibility model that explicitly divides security obligations. AWS is responsible for the security
of
the cloud: physical data centers, the hypervisor, the global network infrastructure, and the hardware underlying managed services. The customer is responsible for security
in
the cloud: OS-level patching for EC2 instances, IAM configuration, encryption of data at rest and in transit, network firewall (Security Group) rules, and application-layer security.

This division shifts depending on the service model. For EC2 (IaaS), customers own everything from the guest OS upward. For RDS (PaaS), AWS manages the OS and database engine patching; the customer manages access controls and encryption. For services like SES or Cognito (SaaS-like), AWS manages almost everything — the customer configures access policies and data handling.
Identity and Access Management
IAM is the primary security control plane for AWS. Every API call is authenticated (who are you?) and authorized (are you allowed to do this?) through IAM. Key principles:
Least privilege.
Grant only the permissions required to perform the task. Use IAM policies with specific Actions, Resources, and Conditions rather than
"Action": "*"
wildcards.
Use IAM roles, not access keys.
EC2 instances, Lambda functions, and ECS tasks should assume IAM roles rather than embedding access keys in code or environment variables. Roles rotate credentials automatically.
Enable CloudTrail.
AWS CloudTrail logs every API call with the caller's identity, source IP, and parameters. It is the audit trail for detecting unauthorized activity.
Reliability Mechanisms

AWS SLAs define the expected uptime for each service (EC2 is 99.99% for multi-AZ, S3 is 99.9% for availability, 99.999999999% for durability). These SLAs apply to the service infrastructure — they do not protect against application bugs, misconfiguration, or single-AZ deployments that a customer architect chooses to run.

The AWS status dashboard (status.aws.amazon.com) and AWS Health (Personal Health Dashboard) provide real-time and historical incident information. For production systems, enable AWS Health notifications so your team is alerted to events affecting your specific resources, not just the public dashboard.
Mental Model

Think of cloud computing like the electrical grid. Before centralized power generation, every factory had to build and maintain its own generator — expensive, requiring engineering expertise, and sized for peak demand. When public utilities emerged, factories stopped worrying about power generation and simply plugged in. They paid per kilowatt-hour, could draw as much as they needed (up to their service agreement), and let the utility handle reliability, fuel sourcing, and infrastructure maintenance.

Cloud computing does the same for IT infrastructure. The cloud provider is the utility: it manages a massive shared pool of compute, storage, and networking; runs it reliably at scale; and charges per unit consumed. Organizations plug in their workloads, pay for what they use, and focus their engineering on the software that differentiates their product — not on the infrastructure that every company needs to run.

The analogy also captures the trade-offs: you are dependent on the utility (vendor lock-in, availability), your meter is always running (cost visibility is important), and the utility sets the terms of the service (SLAs, pricing changes, service deprecations). Understanding these boundaries is as important as knowing the services themselves.
Key Takeaways
Cloud computing delivers on-demand IT resources over the internet with pay-per-use pricing, enabling elastic scaling without upfront hardware investment.
The three service models — IaaS, PaaS, and SaaS — differ in how much of the infrastructure stack the provider manages versus the customer.

AWS is built on the Nitro System (hardware-offloaded virtualization), a global network of Regions and Availability Zones, and a massive portfolio of managed services across every infrastructure category.
The shared responsibility model defines a clear boundary: AWS secures the infrastructure; customers secure their configurations, data, applications, and access controls.
Key cloud advantages — elasticity, global reach, managed services, and variable cost — are only realized when workloads are architected for the cloud, not simply lifted and shifted from on-premises.
Cloud cost is metered continuously; leaving unused resources running is the most common source of unexpected bills. Billing alerts and tagging policies are essential from day one.
Infrastructure as code (CloudFormation, CDK, Terraform) and the principle of least-privilege IAM are foundational practices for secure, reproducible cloud deployments.
